title: Chapter 13: Two Predators, One Table
content: The Black Cathedral stood at the border of Orsi's territory — a ruined church on a hill, visible for miles in every direction. Its walls were blackened by centuries of fire and neglect. Its roof was gone, leaving the nave open to a sky full of cold stars. Its altar was a slab of volcanic glass, polished to a mirror sheen, reflecting the moonlight like a pool of frozen darkness.
Perfect for ambushes. Even more perfect for watching ambushes unfold.

Kael didn't go alone. He brought the entire Polaris Guard — all eight of them, humans and newly-turned vampires who'd volunteered for the Blood Pact in the three weeks since Dredd's death. Marcus led them, positioning his soldiers in the hills surrounding the Cathedral with crossbows loaded with silver-tipped bolts. Sera was with them, hidden in the shadow of a collapsed bell tower, her golden veins deliberately dimmed beneath a heavy cloak.
"If this goes wrong," Marcus had said, "we're not letting you die alone."
"Appreciated," Kael had replied. "But if it goes right, I need you to stay hidden. Orsi can't know I have an army."
"He has his own army. They're in the treeline. Thirty soldiers, maybe more. I counted them on the way in."
"Then we're even."

The Cathedral was empty when Kael arrived. Moonlight streamed through shattered windows, painting the stone floor in silver and black. Count Orsi stood at the altar, his back to the door, his long thin fingers resting on the volcanic glass like a priest preparing for mass.

He turned when Kael's footsteps echoed through the nave. He was older than Kael had expected — not Dredd-aged, but certainly centuries. His eyes were the color of old gold, and his smile was the kind of smile that had never reached them.
"Viscount Ashworth," Orsi said. His voice was soft, polite, the voice of a man who'd never needed to raise it. "Thank you for coming. Most vampires in your position would have run."
"Running didn't seem productive."
"No. No, I don't suppose it would." Orsi gestured to the altar. "Shall we sit? I find business discussions go better when both parties are comfortable."
"I'll stand."

"As you wish." Orsi's smile didn't waver. "I've heard interesting things about you, Viscount. You killed Dredd at noon — collapsed his ceiling and flooded his throne room with sunlight. You've been freeing humans. Paying them wages. Treating them like..." He paused, searching for the word. "Like people."
"You have a problem with that?"

"I have a problem with change. All old vampires do. Change threatens the Hierarchy, and the Hierarchy is what keeps us alive." Orsi began to pace, his footsteps silent on the stone. "But I'm also a pragmatist. You've done something no vampire has ever done. You've built a power base in six weeks that took Dredd two hundred years. And you've done it with a weapon no one else can use."
"The sun."

"The sun." Orsi stopped pacing and faced him directly. "So here's my offer. You work for me. You eliminate targets I designate — Counts, Viscounts, anyone who threatens my consolidation of the eastern territories. You do it quietly, efficiently, at noon when they think they're safe. In exchange, I protect you from the Tribunal. I keep the Veil intact. I help you climb the ranks without interference. Within a year, you'll be a Count. Within a decade, a Duke."
"And if I refuse?"

Orsi's golden eyes flickered. "If you refuse, I send a full report to Prince Marius. Every detail of your Blood Pact. Every record of your daylight activities. Every name of every human you've freed and every vampire you've turned. The Tribunal will declare you a Veil-breaker. Every Prince in the world will hunt you. And when they catch you — and they will catch you — they'll execute you, your paladin, and every single one of your progeny."
The threat settled in the cold air between them. Kael let it sit. He'd been expecting it, after all. The Spider didn't weave webs without poison at the center.
"You forgot something," he said.
"Oh?"

"The Veil Doctrine works both ways. If you expose me, you expose everything. The Blood Pacts. The daylight immunity. The fact that vampire law has a loophole a Viscount drove a truck through." Kael stepped closer. "You think the Tribunal will thank you for that? You think Marius will reward the Count who showed the world how to break his oldest rules?"
Orsi's smile flickered. Just slightly. Just enough.
"I serve the Prince," he said.

"You serve yourself. Same as every other Count." Kael met his eyes. "So let me make you a counter-offer. Stay out of my way. Don't interfere with my territory, my reforms, or my people. In exchange, I won't share *my* information with the human warlords who'd love to know the exact location of every Count's daytime resting place. Including yours."
The silence that followed was absolute. Even the wind seemed to hold its breath.
Then Orsi laughed. It was a cold sound — the laugh of someone who'd just discovered a worthy opponent.
"You're smarter than Dredd," he said. "That's dangerous."
"I know."
"We're going to be enemies, you and I. Eventually. When you've grown strong enough, you'll come for my territory, and I'll come for yours. That's how this world works."
"Probably."

"But not tonight." Orsi stepped back, melting into the shadows at the edge of the altar. "I'll give you three months. Build your army. Train your soldiers. Reform your little city. Get strong enough to be interesting." His golden eyes were the last thing to fade, twin points of light in the darkness. "And when those three months are up... I'll show you what a real Count can do."
The shadows swallowed him whole.
Kael stood alone in the Cathedral, listening to the wind. He didn't move until he heard Marcus's signal from the hills — a brief flash of silver in the moonlight, the all-clear.
Then he walked out of the ruined church and into the cold night, Sera falling into step beside him as they reached the treeline.
"Three months," she said.
"Three months."
"That's not a lot of time."
"It's longer than Dredd had." Kael looked back at the Black Cathedral, a dark silhouette against the stars. "Let's not waste it."
